methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $96M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 9 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
Arbor Energy is a Series A climate and energy company founded by Brad Hartwig that has raised $96M to date. The comparable universe in Climate & Energy consists of 9 peers: 1 public company (LNZA) and 8 private firms including Helion, Redwood Materials, QuantumScape, KoBold Metals, and Electric Hydrogen. However, the peer set lacks sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables—zero companies met the narrow filters for valuation-by-multiple methodology—precluding a reliable peer-median approach.

Stage-progression analysis is the applicable methodology here. Using total capital raised as a proxy for Series A valuation ($96M) and applying a standard 2.0x step-up multiplier typical for progression to the next financing stage yields $192M. This method reflects the expected valuation increase between Series A and subsequent growth stages, anchored to observed stepping patterns in frontier-tech financings.

The triangulation supports a valuation range of $163M to $221M, with a median of $192M. The range reflects a ±15% sensitivity band around the stage-progression result. The primary uncertainty is the absence of tight peer comparables; while the broader Climate & Energy sector contains nine relevant companies, none clustered densely enough around Arbor's stage and scale to provide independent validation. Any material new information on revenue, deployment milestones, or comparable funding rounds in narrower subsectors would warrant recalibration of this estimate.
